content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : KATHRYN DONOVAN
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 410

Date : January 31, 1990

File No.:

Subject : CONFLICT OF INTERESTS

From : Department of Corporations
OFFICE OF POLICY
1107 - 9th Street, 8th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814


On behalf of Christine W. Bender, Commissioner of Corporations
("Commissioner"), I am requesting an informal advice letter from
the staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission with respect
to any possible violations of the Political Reform Act resulting
from the hiring of an independent, outside counsel to be paid by
the Department of Corporations ("Department") in connection with
pending litigation resulting from the Commissioner's approval of
a public debt offering of subordinated debentures issued by
American Continental Corporation ("ACC"). Due to the urgency of
this matter, I request an early opinion and am available to
respond to any request for additional information necessary to
receive your informal advice letter.

The Commissioner is the head of the Department, which administers
numerous laws, including the Corporate Securities Law of 1968
(Corporations Code Sections 25000 et seq., "CSL"), the Franchise
Investment Law, the Knox-Keene Health Care Service Plan Act of
1975, the California Credit Union Law, the Industrial Loan Law,
the Consumer and Commercial Lending Laws, and the Escrow Law.

FACTS

In 1988, the Commissioner, after consulting with attorneys and
examiners on her staff, allowed to go effective one application
for qualification by coordination (with the federal Securities
and Exchange Commission) filed under Section 25111 of the CSL by
ACC to sell subordinated debentures to the public. ACC is the
parent of Lincoln Savings and Loan Association and a second
application was allowed to go effective by such counsel acting
under authority granted by the Commissioner. Lincoln Savings and
Loan Association has been seized by federal regulators. ACC is
currently in bankruptcy proceedings. The purchasers and holders
of the subordinated debentures have initiated a class action suit


ADM 547

KATHRYN DONOVAN                    -2-                 January 31, 1990


against the State of California in the Superior Court of Orange
County because the securities have now been determined to be of
little value.

     The Commissioner did not deny the effectiveness of the
qualification of the ACC securities because of her inability to
find, on the basis of the then current information, that the
proposed plan of business of ACC or the proposed issuance or sale
of the subordinated debentures was not fair, just or equitable,
or that ACC did not intend to transact its business fairly and
honestly, or that the subordinated debentures proposed to be
issued or the method to be used in issuing them would tend to
work a fraud upon the purchasers thereof, all as required by the
CSL.  See Section 25140(a).

     Certain employees of the Department were involved in making
recommendations with respect to the ACC application.  Officials and
other employees have also been involved, in their official
capacity, in activities after ACC declared bankruptcy, including
testimony before legislative committees.  The Commissioner and
various members of her staff have testified periodically before
committees and subcommittees of the California Legislature as well
as Congress.  The Commissioner and several staff members of the
Department now have been subpoenaed for depositions in connection
with the pending class action lawsuit.

     The Commissioner proposes to hire an independent, outside counsel
from a law firm to represent her and certain employees in
connection with the class action suit and any other actions which may
be brought in connection with the offer and sale of the
subordinated debentures.  Neither the Commissioner nor any member
of her staff, to the Commissioner's knowledge, will have a
"financial interest" in the law firm, as defined in Section 87103
of the Political Reform Act.

QUESTIONS PRESENTED

     1.   Does the relationship between the independent, outside
counsel and/or the law firm and the Department present
any potential conflict of interests, of which the
counsel and/or the law firm should be aware, which
would require the counsel and/or the law firm to
disqualify itself from representing other clients
before the Department with respect to any matters?

     2.   Does the Political Reform Act require that the
Commissioner and those Department employees involved in
the ACC matter disqualify themselves from participating
in their official capacity in any matters involving the
independent, outside counsel and/or the law firm,

KATHRYN DONOVAN                    -3-                    January 31, 1990


     including individuals of that law firm or groups of
     individuals of that law firm whose specialty or area of
     practice involves matters subject to the jurisdiction of the
     Department, but which are not in any way connected by the
     above-mentioned ACC legal action to be handled by the
     independent, outside counsel and/or the law firm?

     As indicated above, due to the urgency of this matter, I am
     requesting expedited treatment of this request. If I can be of
     any assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me. In my
     absence, please contact Robert M. Arhelger, Senior Corporations
     Counsel, Office of Policy of this Department. Mr. Arhelger's
     telephone number is (916) 322-3675.


                    [signature]

     WILLIAM KENEFICK
     Assistant Commissioner
     Office of Policy
     (916) 322-3663

     WK:nd

2. Does the Political Reform Act require that the Commissioner and those Department employees involved in the ACC matter disqualify themselves from participating in their official capacity in any matters involving the independent, outside counsel and / or the law firm, including individuals of that law firm or groups of individuals of that law firm whose specialty or area of practice involves matters subject to the jurisdiction of the Department, but which are not in any way connected by the above-mentioned ACC legal action to be handled by the independent, outside counsel and/ or the law firm?

sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether hiring an independent law firm to represent the Commissioner and Department employees in a pending class action lawsuit presents conflicts of interest that would require the law firm to disqualify itself from other Department matters, and whether the Commissioner and staff must recuse themselves from matters involving that law firm unrelated to the ACC litigation.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter dated January 31, 1990, not an FPPC response. Document shows OCR quality issues in header (Cclifornlc, Memorondum). No conclusion/advice from FPPC is present. Requester is William Kenefick requesting expedited treatment.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the California Department of Corporations seeking informal FPPC advice regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from the Commissioner's proposal to hire outside counsel to represent herself and Department employees in a class action lawsuit related to a controversial ACC securities approval decision.
